coredata.dc:title: Impacts of surface chemistry of functional carbon nanodots on the plant growth
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Qiong
Chen, Long
Nie, Xiangkun
Man, Han
Guo, Zhijiang
Wang, Xiuli
Tu, Jiangping
Jin, Gong
Ci, Lijie
coredata.dc:description: Functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) with multiple chemical groups have great impact on the growth regulation of plants. To understand the role of the chemical groups, FCNs were reduced from the raw material by pyrolysis method and hydrolysis method. The chemical structure of these materials were characterized by using TGA, TEM, FT-IR, XPS, Raman and elementary analysis. The raw and reduced FCNs were used as plants growth regulators in culture medium of Arabidopsis thaliana. Our results indicate there is a strong correlation between the physiological responses of plants and the surface chemistries (especially carboxyl group and ester group) of the nanomaterials. The quantum-sized FCNs with multiple carboxyl groups and ester groups show better aqueous dispersity and can induce various positive physiological responses in Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings compared with the FCNs decorated without carboxyl and ester as well as aggregated FCNs. The raw FCNs present higher promotion capacity in plants biomass and roots length, and the quantum-sized FCNs are easier to be absorbed by plants and generate more positive effects on plants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface chemistry
Functional carbon nanodots
Pyrolysis method
Carboxyl group
Ester group
Arabidopsis thaliana

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pyrolysis of FCNs at different temperature. a, TGA curve of FCNs. b, Raman spectra of pyrolytic FCNs. c, TEM pictures of FCNs and pyrolytic FCNs at 200 °C, 350 °C and 500 °C.

Structural characterization of pyrolytic FCNs at different temperature. a, FT-IR spectra of pyrolytic FCNs. b - e, C1s XPS spectra of raw FCNs and pyrolytic FCNs at 200 °C, 350 °C and 500 °C. f - i, O1s XPS spectra of raw FCNs and pyrolytic FCNs.
The diagram of the experimental conditions in the main research. The speculative chemical structure of the FCNs of various surface chemistries were proposed in the diagram.

Regulation effects on plants growth by pyrolytic FCNs at different temperature. a, Dry biomass of 5-day old Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings. The grey bars and white bars are mass of roots and rosettes, respectively. b, Dry biomass of 13-day old Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings. The grey bars and white bars are mass of roots and rosettes, respectively. c, Main roots length of 5-day old Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings. d, Main roots length of 13-day old Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings. e, Phenotype of 5-day old Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings. f, Phenotype of 13-day old Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings. *, P < 0.05; **, P < 0.01; ***, P < 0.001.

head.ce:title: Impacts of surface chemistry of functional carbon nanodots on the plant growth

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Liquid-Solid Structural Evolution & Processing of Materials (Ministry of Education)
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) with multiple chemical groups have great impact on the growth regulation of plants. To understand the role of the chemical groups, FCNs were reduced from the raw material by pyrolysis method and hydrolysis method. The chemical structure of these materials were characterized by using TGA, TEM, FT-IR, XPS, Raman and elementary analysis. The raw and reduced FCNs were used as plants growth regulators in culture medium of Arabidopsis thaliana. Our results indicate there is a strong correlation between the physiological responses of plants and the surface chemistries (especially carboxyl group and ester group) of the nanomaterials. The quantum-sized FCNs with multiple carboxyl groups and ester groups show better aqueous dispersity and can induce various positive physiological responses in Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings compared with the FCNs decorated without carboxyl and ester as well as aggregated FCNs. The raw FCNs present higher promotion capacity in plants biomass and roots length, and the quantum-sized FCNs are easier to be absorbed by plants and generate more positive effects on plants.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) have attracted increasing interests in modern plant biology and biotechnology for their impact on physiological response of plants (Chutipaijit et al., 2017; Lowry et al., 2019; Peng et al., 2020). It has been reported that carbon nanoparticles (including carbon nanotubes, fullerene, carbon nanodots, graphene oxide, etc.) are beneficial for the delivery of nutrient molecules into plant cells and the improvement of pesticides and herbicides application for disease prevention (Hamdi et al., 2015; Lahiani et al., 2015; Adisa et al., 2019; Kah et al., 2019). Some researches have demonstrated that carbon nanomaterials can penetrate the cell walls and membranes and influence the expression of water channel protein (Khodakovskaya et al., 2009; Kabiri et al., 2017). Interestingly, there are so far two opposing results produced in the bio-regulation process of carbon nanomaterials. One is that CNMs have some beneficial effects on plants growth and development, while the other is that CNMs have strong toxic effect on plants. In spite of numerous reports focusing on explaining the mechanism of the effects of carbon nanomaterials on plants, the relationship between carbon nanomaterials and plants biology is complex and still unclear now. There are some evidences of the important role that the surface chemistry of carbon nanomaterials act on plants physiology. In Villagarcia's research (Villagarcia et al., 2012), the negatively charged carbon nanotubes decorated with more functional groups can not only increase seeds germination rate and seedlings biomass, but also active the production of water channel protein. Some other researches (Khodakovskaya et al., 2011; Tripathi et al., 2011) also proved that the negatively charged carbon nanotubes have stronger effects on plants growth and development comparing with both non-functional CNTs and positively charged CNTs. Non-functionalized carbon nanotubes can induce much longer roots than nanotubes functionalized with poly-3-aminobenzenesulfonic acid (Kerfahi et al., 2015). The presence and the type of carbon nanotubes can significantly influence pesticide availability to lettuce seedlings, which was reflected in the fact that amino-functionalized carbon nanotubes induced pesticide content in roots and shoots while non-functionalized carbon nanotubes showed negative impact (Hamdi et al., 2015). Besides, the morphology of the materials, such as size, length, diameter and agglomeration, can also affect the physiological response of plants (Zhai et al., 2015; Lahiani et al., 2016). Given the fact that increasing scale of engineered nanomaterials have been spread into the environment, it is crucial to understand the complex effects that carbon nanomaterials have on the physiology and development of plants systems.

Regulation effects of carbon nanodots on plants have been described as inducing plants growth, development and gene expression in small dose in some early research (Tripathi and Sarkar, 2015; Li et al., 2019). Our early research also proved that the functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) with multiple organic functional groups could influence plant growth and development by enhancing bioaccumulation efficiency and tolerance for heavy metal in Arabidopsis thaliana (Chen et al., 2020a,b). In this report, we focus on understanding the complex roles that the surface chemistry and size of FCNs play in the physiological response of the model plant, Arabidopsis thaliana.

The raw FCNs used in this research were prepared by an electrochemistry method (Ming et al., 2012). Two graphite rode (99.9%, Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd.) were inserted into deionized water as the anodes. A direct current power of 15 V were supplied for 120 h. Then the solution filtered and centrifuged at 10000 rpm to remove impurities. The last obtained solution was freeze-dried for further use.

Murashige & Skoog medium (MS medium) was prepared by the formula of Murashige and Skoog (1962) (Murashige and Skoog, 1962). All the other reagents were analytical grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals were used without further purification.
The Arabidopsis thaliana (Columbia wild type) seeds were non-GMO and provided by Beijing Xinna international Hi-Tech Material Co., Ltd, Beijing, China.

The modified FCNs with different chemical surface were synthesized by thermal decomposition and alkaline hydrolysis of the raw FCNs. The raw FCNs powders were loosely placed in quartz boats and pyrolyzed in tube furnace at temperature 200 °C, 350 °C and 500 °C under an Ar atmosphere. After fully thermal decomposition, FCNs-200, FCNs-350 and FCNs-500 were prepared. To obtain the hydrolytic FCNs, the raw FCNs were sonicated in 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution for 2 h. The mixtures were dialyzed in 3500 D dialysis bag for 3 days to remove free alkaline liquor, the DI water should be changed every 8 h. Then the hydrolytic FCNs (HFCNs) solution was freeze-dried to powders. All the products were stored in a dryer and kept away from water.

Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on Thermo Fisher TGA1 from 35 °C to 800 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 at an Ar flow of 50 mL min−1. The micro-morphology of FCNs were examined using transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-1-11, Japan). FCNs were dispersed in DI water or DMF in ultrasonic machine and dropped on 200-mesh carbon coated copper grid, then dried at 40 °C in an oven. Element analysis was characterized on vario MICRO cube Elementar by using burning method. Imperfect graphene layers of materials were inspected by using Raman spectrometer (invia, Renishaw, Britain). The chemical groups of the FCNs were measured using Bruker Tensor 37 FT-IR (Fourier transform infrared spectroscope) spectrometer in the range from 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1 with 16 scans at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The samples were homogeneously milled with dried anhydrous KBr in a proportion of 1:50 and then pressed the mixed powder at 10 t cm−2 to make a translucent tablet for FT-IR test. X-ray photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is another method to characterize chemical composition and functional groups of the few uppermost layers of FCNs. XPS spectra of FCNs were recorded on Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al anode (Al Kα = 1486.6 eV) at a reduced power of 75 W. The pressure in the analysis chamber was maintained from 6 × 10−7 to 2 × 10−6 Pa when measuring. The higher resolution scan of C1s and O1s spectra were performed over the ranges of 275–300 eV and 522–547 eV, respectively.

Arabidopsis thaliana seeds were vernalized at 1–2 °C before cultivating. The vernalized seeds were sterilized by 10% (Wt. %) sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) solution, and then rinsed 3–5 times with DI water to remove the extra NaClO. Arabidopsis thaliana seeds were placed on half-strength MS (1/2 MS) culture medium in a growth chamber in the condition of 16h light/8h dark, 150 μmol m−2 s−1, day and night temperature 24 °C/22 °C and about 50% humidity. The seeds were grown on 1/2 MS agar medium for 13 days, and then the young seedlings of uniform size (3 seedlings per Petri dish) were transplanted on the experimental and the control culture medium. In the experimental groups, Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings were exposed to raw FCNs, FCNs-200, FCNs-350, FCNs-500 and hydrolyzed FCNs, respectively. Before introducing into the culture medium, the materials were sonicated for at least 2 h to obtain homogeneous dispersion. All used FCNs were supplemented to culture medium in the same concentration (4 mg L−1) in each group. The control group was performed without FCNs addition. All the seedlings cultivated in a climate incubator for 5 days and 13 days for the further test and measurement. Each treatment and the control group applied a completely randomized design with three replicates.

In the supplemental experiment, oxalic acid and trimethyl oxalate were added in 1/2 MS medium. The concentration of carboxyl and ester groups in the supplemental experiment groups were equal to the raw FCNs treated medium. The control group was performed with pure 1/2 MS agar medium. The seedlings (3 seedlings per Petri dish) were cultivated in a climate incubator for 13 days. All experiments and the control group applied a completely randomized design with three replicates.

Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings cultivated for 5 days or 13 days were carefully rinsed twice with DI water to remove particulates and then blotted dry. Development of the seedlings grown on each medium was monitored by photographs. Main root length was measured using a caliper. Then the Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings were separated into roots and rosettes (shoots/leaves). The seedlings were fixed at 105 °C and subsequently dried at 70 °C until constant mass in an oven. Biomass of roots and rosettes were weighed and documented.

Statistical differences were determined by analysis of variance (ANOVA) with control and treatments, and presented as mean ± standard error of three replicates. Statistically significant difference was separated using Duncan's multiple range test at 0.05 probability level.

It was hypothesized that various chemical groups (such as carboxyl, ester, hydroxyl, etc.) on the surface of FCNs would significantly influence the aggregation of the materials and affect their ability to regulate plants growth and development, including biomass accumulation, roots elongation, rosettes mass, etc. Therefore, several types of FCNs decorated with different functional groups were prepared to study the regulation effects of surface chemistry on plant growth and development.

Groups of FCNs with different surface chemical properties were prepared by using thermal decomposition treatment from raw FCNs without other reagent addition, in order to avoid introducing impurities. It was reported that the amount of the functional groups on the surface of activated carbon could be characterized and quantitatively determined by temperature-programmed desorption (TPD) curve (Figueiredo et al., 1999). When the carbon-based materials are thermal-treated under inert atmosphere, oxygen chemical groups would be selectively removed at certain temperatures. However, the assignments of the degradation temperature to specific surface groups are confused in some research, because the degradation temperature may be affected by the geometry and chemical structure of the materials, the heating rate and the heating equipment (Figueiredo et al., 1999).

Fig. 1 (a) presents the thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) curve of the raw FCNs. Surface functional groups on FCNs decompose upon heating by releasing CO and CO2 at different temperatures (Figueiredo et al., 1999). The relative weight continuously decreases when the temperature rises from 35 °C to 800 °C. From the differential of the TGA curve (supplemental data Fig. S1), the relative weight decreases at six temperature intervals. At these temperature intervals of 35°C–125 °C, 125°C–216 °C, 216°C–353 °C, 353°C–502 °C, 502°C–650 °C, the decreases percentage of the relative value are 10%, 11%, 20%, 18%, 7% and 16%, respectively. Theoretically, the adsorbed water is dried below 100 °C and the graphite structure is destroyed beyond 600 °C (Figueiredo et al., 1999). As a result, temperatures of 200 °C, 350 °C, 500 °C are applied to reduce specific functional groups on FCNs in this research. The speculation is proved by Raman spectra (Fig. 1b), as Raman test is a powerful tool to indicate the graphitization degree of the FCNs. The ratios between the intensities of the G and D peaks (ID/G) in Raman spectra can reflect the defect level of FCNs. By calculating, the ID/G of FCNs, FCNs-200, FCNs-350 and FCNs-500 are 0.58, 0.48, 0.46 and 1.00, respectively. As determined by the ID/G, the surface chemical groups partly reduced at 200 °C and altered at 350 °C with the stable carbon skeleton. However, the ID/G of FCNs-500 increased sharply, revealing the carbon skeleton of FCNs is broken when thermal treated at 500 °C. The changes in functional groups of FCNs can be proved by further surface chemistry characterization with FT-IR and XPS. Because functional groups can affect the hydrophilicity and bioactivity of FCNs and further influence the regulation effect of FCNs on plants growth and development (Spielman-Sun et al., 2019), higher temperatures over 500 °C are not investigated in this research.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images indicate the dimensional and morphological change of various FCNs (Fig. 1c–e). While the raw FCNs are nanodots of 2–8 nm, the FCNs-200 are homogeneous dots of 1–2 nm. However, the size of the materials increases with the rising treated temperature, probably due to the agglomeration of nanomaterials. The FCNs-350 are heterogeneous dots of 4–8 nm, and FCNs-500 are large agglomerations. Based on optical observations, the dispersion abilities of these nanomaterials in water is obviously different, and the raw FCNs and FCNs-200 have the best dispersity, while FCNs-500 is difficult to disperse in water (supplemental data Fig. S2). Both surface chemistry and particle size can affect the dispersity of carbon-based nanomaterials (Zhai et al., 2015; Lahiani et al., 2016), and the hydrophilicity of carbon nanomaterials has significant impact on plants growth and development.

The element analysis of the raw and reduced FCNs (samples FCNs-200, FCNs-350, FCNs-500) are shown in Table 1 . It is apparent that pyrolysis treatment lead to a substantial decrease in the oxygen and hydrogen content of FCNs, while carbon content increase in this process. The decrease in the amount of surface oxygen groups is evidenced by the decrease of the ratio of oxygen to carbon (Ot/Ct). Fourier transform infrared spectroscope (FT-IR) spectra in Fig. 2 a qualitatively reflect the change of functional groups on FCNs. The raw FCNs have the greatest diversity of chemical groups, including carboxyl and carboxylic anhydride (1728 cm−1), ester and lactone (around 1720 cm−1), hydroxyl (around 3500 cm−1), CC bond (1644 cm−1), ether bond (around 1250 cm−1) and alkyl group (around 3000 cm−1). The band at 1728 cm−1, which is associated with carboxyl and carboxylic anhydride, disappears when heating to 200 °C (FCNs-200). Meanwhile, the relative intensity of the peak at 3500 cm−1 to that at 1644 cm−1 decreased dramatically. The changes demonstrate that carboxyl on FCNs are reduced at 200 °C. Besides, the decreased intensity of hydroxyl absorption peak (around 3500 cm−1) result in the other peaks intensities relatively enhancing, such as the peak of ester at 1714 cm−1 and the peaks of C–H below 1000 cm−1. The peaks related to ester and lactone become weak and split into two shoulder peaks (1721 cm−1 and 1791 cm−1) when FCNs are thermally treated at 350 °C (FCNs-350), which indicates that the ester reduced during the pyrolysis process and probably partially reacted into carbonyl. Due to the pyrolysis of a large number of the surface functional groups, the alkyl at the edge of carbon skeleton were removed, resulting in the significantly decreased intensity of the peaks below 1000 cm−1. The peaks associated with ester groups around 1720 cm−1 totally disappear at 500 °C (FCNs-500), revealing the completely pyrolysis of ester. Besides, the carbon skeleton may be broken because the peak intensity of CC at 1645 cm−1 (corresponding to the peak at 1623 cm−1 in FCNs-350) decreased obviously. Fig. 2 (b - e) and (f - i) show the C1s and O1s spectra obtained by X-ray photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS results can quantitively reflect the elements contents and chemical bonds of FCNs. The peaks are deconvoluted by a sum of Lorentzian-Gaussian functions, and the amounts of chemical groups are calculated from the corresponding peak areas divided by the appropriate sensitivity factors (supplemental data Table S1). Fig. 2 (c) shows the –COO-, C–O-R(H), CO and sp2 C on FCNs-200, which are assigned from the peaks located at 288.4 eV, 286.5 eV, 285.8 eV and 284.9 eV, respectively. FCNs-350 have the similar carbon bonds as FCNs-200, in addition to the carbide at 282.4 eV. However, the ratio of –COO- to sp2 C on FCNs-350 (20.5 : 100) is significantly lower than that of FCNs-200 (42.0 : 100), revealing the decomposition of -COO- at 350 °C. The results of FCNs-200 and FCNs-350 are consistent with the corresponding O1s spectra except the chemisorbed water at 535.9 eV. From Fig. 2 (e) and (i), the –COO- groups completely disappear on FCNs-500. There remain only C–O–C (H) and CO bonds on FCNs-500. The results of FT-IR and XPS also prove the changes in Raman spectra.

From the above results, it is concluded that heating up to 200 °C removes most of the carboxylic groups on FCNs. A part of ester groups are removed at 350 °C and all of the ester groups have be decomposed at 500 °C. There are only carbonyl, hydroxyl and alkyl groups left on FCNs-500. The chemical groups on each material are exhibited in Fig. 3 . Rich hydrophilic functional groups and small diameter endow the raw FCNs and FCNs-200 with high dispersity in aqueous. The lower dispersity in water of FCNs-350 and FCNs-500 is caused by both the decoration of alkyl groups and the aggregation of molecular. The presence of a large number of nonpolar functional groups (alkyl and ether) makes FCNs-500 insoluble with polar solvents, and the intermolecular dispersion forces further cause serious intermolecular aggregation, which lead to the low water-solubility of FCNs-500.

To determine the physiological response of plants upon the application of FCNs with specific surface properties and levels of agglomeration, the growth and development of Arabidopsis thaliana were monitored through tissue culture on half Murashige and Skoog (1/2 MS) medium supplemented with four types of FCNs (Fig. 3). The medium without FCNs was set as the control (group CK). The raw FCNs have the richest carboxyl groups and ester groups. FCNs-200 have less carboxyl groups than the raw FCNs and have more ester groups than FCNs-350. FCNs-500 have the least amount of the two groups. Consequently, experiments conducted to test the physiological responses of plants with four types of FCNs can demonstrate the effect of carboxyl and ester groups on plants growth and development (Fig. 3).

The cultured Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings were harvested at 5 days and 13 days. This research highlights the most significant (***, P < 0.001) increase in the dry biomass accumulation of 5-day Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings exposed to raw FCNs and FCNs-200 with the highest dispersity in water. The FCNs-350 increase the biomass of seedlings significantly (*, P < 0.05), while FCNs-500 supplement is ineffectual on promoting plants growth compared with the control group (Fig. 4 a). Although the biomass increase of 13-day Arabidopsis seedlings treated by raw FCNs is still the most significant, the promotion effects of FCNs-200 and FCNs-350 on biomass are fading (Fig. 4b). FCNs-350 and FCNs-500 do not show significant influence on biomass accumulation of 13-day seedlings. A similar trend can be observed in the main root elongation of experimental seedlings (Fig. 4 c and d). The raw FCNs, FCNs-200, FCNs-350 all induce main root elongation on 5-day Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings, however, only raw FCNs show positive effect on main root of 13-day seedlings. Fig. 4e and f shows that the raw FCNs treated seedlings have longer and stronger roots, bigger rosette and thicker leaves, while other treated seedlings are rather weak.

Consequently, the Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings grown on the culture medium supplemented with raw FCNs exhibit the most active growth compared with seedlings exposed to the other types of FCNs and the control group, which can be explained by the richest multiple surface chemical groups. Although the seedlings cultivated on medium supplemented with FCNs-200 are significantly more flourishing than the control group, they are weaker than the raw FCNs treated seedlings. It is hypothesized that the decrease of biomass and root length on FCNs-200 treated plants can be explained by the lack of carboxyl groups on the surface of the FCNs. A statistically significant but much lower increase in biomass is demonstrated on young seedlings exposed to FCNs-350, but the promotion effect is underdeveloped with time. This result proves that ester groups at the surface of nanodots play an important role in regulating plants growth. Interestingly, this speculation is further proved by the fact that the seedlings exposed to FCNs-500 do not show any statistically significant difference in growth compared with the control. However, it can't be ignored the fact that the FCNs-500 particles are agglomerated into relatively large clusters, which can decrease aqueous dispersity and lower their capacity for being taken up by the roots systems and therefore has a less obvious impact on plants physiological responses. So further investigation is still necessary to prove the mechanism.

A further experiment is conducted to indicate the correlation between the functional group of FCNs and its physiological responses upon plants. The hydrolyzed FCNs (HFCNs) are prepared from the raw FCNs after hydrolyzing in alkali liquor, dialyzing and freeze-drying. The pH of the final solution is about 6.8, and the size of the nanodots is about 2–10 nm (supplemental data Fig. S3a). The similar ID/G value of FCNs and HFCNs (supplemental data Fig. S3b) reveals the little difference of their defect levels. The FT-IR spectrum reveals that all ester groups are hydrolyzed to carboxyl groups in hydrolyzed FCNs (supplemental data Fig. S3c). The large amount of carboxyl groups endow the hydrolyzed FCNs with high water solubility. From extended date Fig. S4, Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings exposed to hydrolyzed FCNs express significantly (P < 0.001) higher dry biomass compared with the control and significantly (P < 0.001) lower biomass compared with the raw FCNs. The tissue culture results demonstrate that ester groups perform significantly promotion effect on inducing plants growth. Besides, although the positive effect of carboxyl groups on plants can't be ignored in the hydrolyzed FCNs, the combination of ester and carboxyl in the raw FCNs induce much more significant promotion effect.

To further verify the synergistic effect of functional groups and nanostructure of FCNs on plant, this observation is conducted by the experiment on culture medium supplemented with oxalic acid and dimethyl oxalate. The oxalic acid and dimethyl oxalate are small-molecular organic acid and ester, which contain two carboxyl groups and two ester groups, respectively. The supplemented reagents have the equivalent amount of carboxyl and ester groups compared with the raw FCNs. It is dramatic that only dimethyl oxalate can significantly (P < 0.01) promote biomass of the seedlings by about 37.5% (supplemental data Fig. S5), given the fact that ester groups can generate positive effect on plants. We have proved the positive effect of carboxyl groups on plants growth in the above section, this inconsistent result regarding the influence of carboxyl on plants growth may be caused by the nanostructure of FCNs sample. In other word, the microstructure of carbon nanomaterials is also an extremely important factor on activating plants growth and development.

Based on the biological research above, it can be clearly discovered that materials that belong to the same family of carbon nanodots but differ in surface chemistry and micro-morphology can cause diverse physiological responses to plant seedlings. Both of the surface groups (especially carboxyl group and ester group) and nanostructure of FCNs can dramatically affect plants growth and development. Firstly, the hydrophilic and electronegative functional groups on the surface of FCNs can enhance their hydrophilicity and inhibit their agglomeration in water. Secondly, carboxyl groups can increase the adsorption sites on FCNs to enhance the chemical adsorption capacity to nutrient ions or molecules through electrostatic interaction, thus promoting the uptake of nutrients by plants (Qian et al., 2018). We hypothesize that carboxyl and ester groups also can induce plants growth by activating the production of the water channel protein (Villagarcia et al., 2012), which is a key protein for water uptake in roots and plants development. Thirdly, –COOH can also reduce the pH of the rhizosphere environment, which can not only directly promote the absorption of nutrient ions by the root system, but also indirectly affect plant growth by changing the rhizosphere microbial environment (Kibbey et al., 2019). As a result, the oxygen-containing functional groups can affect the promotion effect of FCNs on plants by adjusting pH, hydrophilicity and surface charge. Moreover, FCNs with smaller nano-size have better dispersibility, larger specific surface area, and better physical adsorption capacity to nutrients and plants. Given these hypotheses, modified FCNs with smaller size and more electronegative group can actively regulate plant growth as an exogenous substance. However, further studies are still required to fully understand the mechanism of the effects of carboxyl and ester groups on the physiological responses of plants.

In conclusion, we have found a strong correlation between the physiological responses of plants and the specific properties of the FCNs, including the surface characteristics (type of functional groups), graphene structure as well as the agglomeration and size of the nanomaterials. The most noticeable increase in plants growth is observed on seedlings exposed to the raw FCNs with the richest functional groups and small size, compared with plants exposed to other FCNs and small organic compounds. It can be concluded that the quantum-sized FCNs with multiple of carboxyl groups and ester groups have the most critical impact on promoting seedlings growth, because they have perfect aqueous dispersity, nutrient adsorption capacity and bioaffinity.

Considering the biosafety of the FCNs, the detection of biological uptake and bio-distribution of the FCNs are still a challenging process owing to the limitations of testing methods, both qualitatively and quantitatively. Although TEM, Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence images and photothermal/photoacoustic analysis have been proposed to characterize the existence of carbon nanomaterials (Bitounis et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018), but all of them have advantages and limitations. Moreover, further studies are still required to fully understand the mechanism that the combination of carboxyl and ester groups influence physiological response of plants in molecular level.

Qiong Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft Long Chen: Validation, Investigation Xiangkun Nie: Investigation Han Man: Formal analysis, Data Curation Zhijiang Guo: Visualization, Data Curation, Project administration Xiuli Wang: Resources, Methodology Jiangping Tu: Resources, Writing - Review & Editing Gong Jin: Resources, Project administration Lijie Ci: Validation, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by High-level Talents' Discipline Construction Fund of Shandong University (31370089963078), Shandong Provincial Science and Technology Major Project (2016GGX104001, 2017CXGC1010, 2018JMRH0211), the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University (2016JC005, 2017JC042, 2017JC010), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017MEM002).

ce:section.ce:para:
